front: Philosopher: outlined four methods for settling opinion — tenacity, authority, a priori, and scientific — in an essay that compares tenacity to an ostrich burying its head in sand
back: Charles Sanders Peirce ("The Fixation of Belief")

front: Philosopher: proposed three grades of clearness of apprehension and defined truth as the opinion 'fated to be ultimately agreed to by all who investigate'
back: Charles Sanders Peirce ("How to Make Our Ideas Clear")
work: How to Make Our Ideas Clear

front: Concept: the three categories of firstness (quality/feeling), secondness (relation/reaction), and thirdness (representation/habit), established in 'On a New List of Categories'
work: Semiotics & On a New List of Categories

front: Concept: a sign that represents an object by virtue of a real connection to it — smoke for fire, a weathervane for wind direction
work: Semiotics & On a New List of Categories

front: Concept: the effect of a sign on the person processing it, forming the third element of Peirce's triadic semiotics alongside sign/representamen and object
work: Semiotics & On a New List of Categories

front: Philosopher: developed theories of continuity (synechism) and absolute chance (tychism) arguing that properties of the universe evolved in Darwinian fashion
Philosopher: in 'The Law of Mind,' argued that ideas naturally tend to spread, gaining generality and losing intensity, and that all thought must be fully continuous
Philosopher: argued against social Darwinism by proposing 'agapeism' (evolutionary love) as the true driver of evolution
Philosopher: showed NAND and NOR were Sheffer functions three decades before Sheffer, and formulated a namesake law stating that if 'P then Q' implies P, then P must be true
Philosopher: divided inference into abduction, deduction, and induction, and coined the term 'phaneroscopy' to replace 'phenomenology'

Philosopher: renamed his philosophy 'pragmaticism' after breaking with William James; published extensively in Popular Science Monthly but was banned from Harvard due to bad temper and serial adultery
Philosopher: made the type-token distinction in an article for The Monist, noting that the word 'aardvark' has eight token letters but five type letters
work: Semiotics & On a New List of Categories
